item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could I suggest a possible crosslink with SCP-2020? Because I think I just burst a kidney. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me, it's beautifully clever. There's a treasure trove of follow-ups that could be made, too- military application of imaginary bears???

looks like this utterly unbearable article clawed its way into the list of my favorite articles on this site

i am so not sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a bear's manifestation in or around the subject solving it.

in?

(+1 because duh.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the few articles on this site where I wish I could upvote twice (in fact +bear would be the ideal number). The perfectly clinical tone coupled with the utter absurdity of the subject are a perfect match.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is utterly absurd and I love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saying an article needs to be a -J to be humorous is incredibly narrow-minded. If you don't like it, fair enough, but "this article is funny so it should be a -J" is old, tired and invalid at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who really isn't a big mathematics fan this started off on shaky ground. Then it became an obvious -J article not in tone but in content. The last line sadly sealed its fate as a -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, yes. The perfect way to kill all of my rivals in the mathematician community.

I never knew I needed bear math. +1
Best bear-related SCP on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I want to try integrating 1/x from 0 to SCP-1313.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're missing whole ranges of possibilities, an anti meme is any concept that discourages it's own spread rather than encouraging it, just forgetting about it is a single dull possibility as to how this happens, people who learn about it could cease being affected by gravity, it could be a proof that sharing information in any way is immoral, they could turn into a fish!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm no ursinumerologist, but my gut says either 0, 2, sqrt(2), or undefined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whatever safety measures they used to take the square root of a bear would presumably also work to compute bear2; or bearbear.

But I would hope anyone educated enough to be able to calculate bear fractals and bear-plexes would also be bright enough to realize why they shouldn't do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The threat is mostly localized — in order to raise the bear to a power, you need to start with bear in the first place, in which case there is an angry bear right next to you. Good luck trying to work under those conditions!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be threat level black? There's no known limit to how much bear you can solve for. If you can exponentiate it, you can do any hyperoperation. What happens when it goes through pentation? Or you think about bear times Graham's number? Either the Earth is filled with bear, or a hyperbear eats the Earth, or the fabric of reality tears and something weirder happens. Where does the bear come from? I solve for a large enough coefficient of bear, can I cause heat death by making all of the universe's available energy form into BEAR?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, it's been a year and I still smile whenever this gets brought up. Loved it when it was just a draft, still love it now. +1 root bear times over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I love this article, I have to give it a no vote. I like mathematics too much to be able to wrap my head around this article. Suspension of disbelief was out the window as soon as I gave myself a headache trying to figure out the square root of bear.
How many eyes would it have?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who loves the sort of theoretical side of mathematics. (that sort of "some infinite are biger that others" type stuff), this is amazing. also p funny. definite plus

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ei*SCP-1313 = sin(SCP-1313) + i*cos(SCP-1313)

That's gotta have some military potential

Great skip that toes the line between mainlist and -J-list, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: SCP-2875 is a version of SCP-1313 that got stuck in a loop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Formulations involving scp-1313 times the square root of -1 yield inconsistent results.

Extensive testing has also determined that the majority of bears are irrational, or can be made so by applying the poke-with-a-stick operator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If f(x)=0 is SCP-1313, then x is a bear. Then, I think, y=f(x) would be just a weird graph, and you'd need a bear for the graph to reach 0.

Also, I think that experiment log is lacking. It would be really interesting to see description of irrational and imaginary bears, as well as other opportunities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A computer could probably solve the equation, but you'd end up with a bear appearing — most likely inside the computer itself, damaging it beyond repair. If you tried to draw a graph of y = SCP-1313, you'd get as far as calculating a single point before you got mauled by a bear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that there is a need for more experiments. What if SCP-1313 would be solved numerically? What if it would be solved by the computer system? If SCP-1313 can be written as f(x)=0 what would happen if some CAS would try to draw a graph for y=f(x)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was missing the

« SCP-1312 | SCP-1313 | SCP-1314 »

I added it, I hope that was okay.

item.title: Re: Math can work that way.
item.content:encoded: quantum math bears also makes sense

item.title: Re: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded:
this is probably the best explanation
but in my mind there are quite a few "anomalies" that aren't "anomalous" and simply require a reevaluation of how the universe works
an anomaly by definition is something that simply doesn't work

item.title: Re: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded:
Hmm
Hmmm
so you're saying that this anomaly is operating on a HUMAN level?
this makes a hell of a lot of sense, and i like it, and it has potential, but
iunno
i feel like the point of anomalies is that they're somehow ABOVE or BEYOND humans

One question for example is, if an animal sees 173, does it freeze?
perhaps 173 and 096 only react to "sapient" creatures
2719 suggests that the concept "inside" as "being enveloped by something" means something outside of how we think of it, but it does still think like a human
the lizard is just the lizard is the lizard is the lizard, and it is by definition unkillable.
math though doesn't require ANYTHING to be alive, it just is. before humans there was math as the core concept that it is and after humans it'll still exist in the same old way, and… am i just proving you right? i feel like the abstract concepts you've listed are somehow different from MATH, which is this solid unshakeable THING, and by definition cannot create but can merely demonstrate

item.title: Re: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded: Mathematical operations notably don't ever result in spontaneous physical effects. Magic spells, while they really do exist, significantly fail to produce results above the noise line. In fantasy fiction magic spells can be expected to actually work.

item.title: Re: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded:
Otherwise? You'll soon find yourself trying to describe 173 in terms of photons and momentum, and that way lies irrecoverable madness.

Something I like about this site are the viable story ideas that spring forth from random forum posts.

item.title: Re: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded:
According to Google, Cambridge, and Merriam-Webster, maths is something like the study of numbers. What maths isn't is the numbers themselves. The field doesn't claim to be the representation of logic at a fundamental level; it is, and always has been, how humans and the machines we design interpret that logic. There is likely some ideal MATHEMATICS that is pure and perfect and absolute and independent of us, but it's not our puny mathematics — that poor substitute is just what we do when we muck about with calculators and abacuses and joining up dots on funny bits of paper. The universe doesn't know what a "logical process" is, because (as you said) numbers already exist in their final, abstract state. But the anomaly itself is a logical process, or series of them, and that's where your argument falls down. You can't criticise a work for something it never pretended to represent.

To demonstrate a little further: "line of sight" has no meaning in physics, but we accept 173's existence. We don't ask how 682 knows how to adapt, or how 096 differentiates between faces and non-faces, even at an indistinguishable level. 2719 operates entirely under the assumption that human concepts have real meanings and boundaries — be it Inside or the objects/entities that are becoming or going Inside. None of their anomalies are present at the basic, logical, physical levels — but, judging by your voting history, you're fine with that.

Excepting articles like Minus Two Kilograms, almost every SCP on the site requires some distortion of the universe to allow anomalies to be both logically and narratively consistent. The SCP universe needs us to assign mental categories to things, and then for those categories to have meaning. In order to derive any enjoyment from SCP (beyond, perhaps, debating things in the forums), you need to accept, subconsciously or not, that the way humans perceive things is somehow "special" in the eyes of the universe.

Otherwise? You'll soon find yourself trying to describe 173 in terms of photons and momentum, and that way lies irrecoverable madness.

item.title: Math can work that way.
item.content:encoded:
Sure. 2+2=4. The 4 did not spontaneously appear out of nowhere. But that's just pure mathematics, and this is the real world.

The medium dictates what that "4" represents. To a computer, it could be a memory address or an integer. To me, it could represent a nebulous concept of four-ness which I have developed over years of being told what "four" means. If the number is SCP-1313, it could also mean a bear appears.

And sometimes things DO go into a combiner machine and something comes out: something that wasn't ALWAYS there. There's no way of knowing what'll happen since there are no hidden variables. It's a completely unknowable system until it's measured.

item.title: Re: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded:
Assuming 1313 follows all the logic of normal mathematics

But… it doesn't, and that's kinda the entire point? ¯\_(ツ)_/¯ Like, the entire purpose of the SCP archive is to catalogue all the different ways reality can break. If it fit with our understanding of the universe, it wouldn't be anomalous and it wouldn't be an SCP anymore.

item.title: Math doesn't work that way
item.content:encoded:
No.

Assuming 1313 follows all the logic of normal mathematics, it wouldn't just create a bear out of thin air.
Something some people don't understand is that when you write 2+2=4, you aren't putting 2 and 2 in a combiner machine and 4 pops out. 4 was ALWAYS there. It'd require an already-existing bear to be around. And don't say it teleports a bear from somewhere else when you solve the equation. Values aren't MOVED in math, they just ARE.

-1

item.title: OEIS Sequence A141030, terms 82-88
item.content:encoded:
This is insane. I never imagined anything of mine would get this highly rated. The people who upvoted, the people who downvoted, the people who picked it apart and evaluated it, the people who created an endless stream of bear puns in the comments — I love all of you so much.

My first +300. Hot damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been wondering why this works for me yet 033, an anomaly with roughly the same concept, doesn't, and I think I've come up with an answer: big-dick energy.

For those not in the know, "big-dick energy" defines a state of being where the raw chaos of forging one's own path in life is mixed with the rigid steel of self-determination. It's proudly going to McDonalds to order 16 packets of BBQ sauce and nothing else. It's booty shorts in the dead of winter with "JUICY" proudly printed on the back. It's coldposting your 001.

1313 does not care to hide behind complex mathematics; on the contrary, it revels in simple algebra. There's no attempt to justify its place in the number line; on the contrary, 1313 forces the number line to conform to it. Most importantly, it does not treat the mathematical violation as the main anomaly; that's saved for the mathematically-derived bear.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The square root of a grizzly bear is prime, however, and is the only prime number that a) is not a cardinal number, b) is neither even nor odd, and c) contains an animal component.

The universe is a better place for having this written in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly how you right a funny article without becoming too absurd. It doesn't rely on a goofy interview, an insane backstory, there's no moon wizards or drunk aliens, it's just a legitimate investigation into an interesting thought experiment with a comical result.

+bear, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes!

This is the kind of funny articles I'm looking for! The idea is absolutely ludicrous but the clinical tone makes it strangely believable in the Foundation's universe.

Top 5 you go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[…] this doesn't land for me.

I am left wanting more, and instead all I get is a joke.

These are my main points. Sure there are other explanations or non-explanations for the article, and yes, I am talking about a totally different article here. But, that's the execution that would have made me like this (which you also could have done with your other explanations you gave). Clearly it's fine that that wasn't the execution you went with, because people like it, so it lived, but I have explained what would have made me like the article.

Presumptuous: Adj. : (of a person or their behavior) failing to observe the limits of what is permitted or appropriate

Synonyms: brazen, overconfident, arrogant, bold, audacious, forward, familiar, impertinent, insolent, impudent, cocky […]

I don't think calling that presumptuous was necessary.

EDIT: Plus, I might add, “imagined qualities” are, like, half of reading something. Interpretations. Using your mind’s eye is part of reading, comprehending, and understanding. Writing, this site especially, uses implications to evoke conclusions the reader comes to themselves, and it’s good practice, and expected. I don’t see much merit to dismissing “imagined qualities”.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna be honest, this is really bizarre, definetely one of the weirdest things on this site. When it starts to make sense, lines like "the set of all real numbers that are not animals" appear; and I love it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely terrific - a silly concept, but the execution is played so dry and straight, and it is taken beautifully to its illogical conclusion. The result is a series of memorable and hilarious lines - for me this is one of the funniest things on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes I think this might be the best article on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read this article, I think….20 times, at least.

This is the single best example of the effective use of clinical tone I've ever read.

I wish I could upvote things twice.
A huge thanks to everyone who read or voted on this. I can't express how happy/confused/terrified it makes me that this of all things is my highest rated article. Thank you all <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So now I have a new headcanon.
Math is fundamentally broken in SCPverse, hence the anomalies. Hence this "2+2 equals bear" thing and a missing integer between 5 and 6. Should math be returned to its proper state, all anomalies would cease to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I found my new favorite SCP. +1, but only because I can't give this +Grizzly Bear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a bear's manifestation in or around the subject solving

God fucking dammit, take your +1.

Glad someone caught it ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ugh, formatting. nevermind

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
∫∞0(1−12t2)(1+4t2)3∫∞1/2log|ζ(σ+it)| dσ dt=π(3−γ)32

is the bear equation. If you get this reference I applaud you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Make you come out of your shell of dormancy once again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never said it was a "universal constant". Perhaps we live in a bubble of warped logic where the equation produces the conceptual ur-type of "grizzly bear" (chosen randomly from the infinite range of metaphysical constructs) instead of, say, 15. Whatever caused this bubble (even if it had a cause, which most anomalies don't), the end result is the same. Or, it could be universal. Whether I write a six-thousand word treatise on the nature of mathematical anomalies or not, the end result is exactly what's presented in this article — that it was the bears that implanted themselves into maths somehow, and that it's a universal anomaly, are pretty big assumptions to make. Yes it's silly, because I took a joke and made an article out of it; all there is to the anomaly is what is presented here, no more and no less. It's exactly as big and world-shattering as you, the reader, have taken for granted that it is.

What you're asking here (it seems, anyway) is for me to write an entirely different article in which I explore the deeper implications that you created for the anomaly. It's fine if you don't find it funny, or dislike the style in general, but reinforcing it with imagined qualities like that just seems a little… presumptuous, I guess. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tried latex for the root one, but honestly thought using the ol' CSS overline hack worked better — latex has a habit of distorting things a bit, and stands out too much for my tastes. I initially used copy-pasted characters for the R∖A thing, but they didn't show up for certain users. I'll see if that works better, and implement it if it does :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WHAT HAVE YOU DONE?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guess the author may use $\LaTeX$ to type the math formulas. For $\mathbb{R}\backslash\mathbb{A}$ and $\sqrt{\text{SCP-1313}}$, the codes are

[[$ \mathbb{R}\backslash\mathbb{A} $]]
[[$ \sqrt{\text{SCP-1313}} $]]

Can we get a polar bear if we solve the equation in polar coordinates?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reason I up voted is that reading this article made me feel like A math teacher was explaining math rules again except with grizzly bears…That appear and … kill you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's "Digimon are the champions". It's hard to hear, which kind of sucks, because it's a pretty kickass theme.

(Though the Digimon Adventure tri. version of Butter-Fly and Digimon Tamer's Biggest Dreamer are better.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a reddit-tier punchline. It falls into "LOLRANDOM" territory.

How so? It’s a perfectly reasonable warning based on the effects of the SCP.

Maybe you misunderstood due to the phrasing, but "bear-related injury" isn't a threat by the administration. It's a genuine possibility that could arise if someone accidentally conjures a bear by overthinking the anomaly.
